text: In the spring, the season when kings led their armies out to war, David sent Joab with his officers and all Israel to destroy the Ammonites and besiege Rabbah, but David himself stayed behind in Jerusalem.
Late one afternoon David got up from resting and walked on the roof of the palace, and from there he saw a woman bathing who was very beautiful.
He sent someone to find out who she was, and was told she was Bathsheba, daughter of Eliam and wife of Uriah the Hittite.
David sent messengers to bring her to him; she had just finished purifying herself after her monthly period, and he slept with her before she went back home.
She became pregnant and sent word to David that she was carrying his child.
So David sent a message to Joab telling him to send Uriah the Hittite to him.
When Uriah arrived, David asked him how Joab was doing, how the troops were doing, and how the war was going.
Then David told Uriah to go home and rest, and sent a gift of food after him from the palace.
But Uriah did not go home; instead he slept at the entrance of the palace with the rest of the king's guard.
When David learned Uriah had not gone home, he asked him why, since he had just come back from a journey.

Uriah answered that the ark and the armies of Israel and Judah were camped out in the open field, and he could not bring himself to enjoy his own house, his wife, and comfort while his fellow soldiers were exposed like that.
David told him to stay one more day and he would send him back to the army the next day, so Uriah remained in Jerusalem that day and the following one.
David invited him to eat and drink until Uriah was drunk, but that night Uriah again slept among the guards rather than going home.
The next morning David wrote a letter to Joab and had Uriah carry it back with him.
In it he instructed Joab to put Uriah at the front of the fiercest fighting and then pull back the support around him, so that he would be struck down and killed.
So as Joab kept up the siege, he assigned Uriah to a spot where he knew the enemy's strongest fighters were stationed.
The men of the city came out and fought, and some of David's soldiers fell, including Uriah the Hittite.
Joab sent David a full report of how the battle had gone.
He instructed his messenger on exactly what to say once he had finished the report.
If the king got angry and asked why the troops had gone so close to the wall, knowing they would be shot at from above,
the messenger was to remind him how Abimelech died at Thebez when a woman dropped a millstone on him from the wall, and then add that Uriah the Hittite had died too.
So the messenger went and reported to David everything Joab had told him to say.
He said the enemy had come out against them in the open field, and they had driven them back to the entrance of the city gate.
But archers on the wall had shot down at the servants, killing some of the king's men, and Uriah the Hittite was among the dead.

David told the messenger to tell Joab not to be troubled by it, since the sword claims one man as easily as another, and to press the attack on the city harder until it fell — meant to steady Joab's nerves.
When Uriah's wife heard that her husband had died, she mourned for him.
When the time of mourning ended, David had her brought to his house, and she became his wife and bore him a son. But what David had done was evil in the LORD's sight.
